coredata.dc:title: Improving dynamic parameter estimation in electric vehicle batteries: A comparative study of FFRLS methods with Open Circuit Voltage assumptions
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown Copyright © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Mohamed, Mohamed A.A.
Du Gard, Joseph
Yu, Tung Fai
Grandjean, Thomas
coredata.dc:description: Accurate battery modelling is crucial for Battery Management Systems (BMS) to ensure precise state estimation and dynamic characteristic prediction, optimizing power and energy management in Electric Vehicles (EVs). Among various models, the Electric Equivalent Circuit Model (EECM) is widely used due to its ability to represent dynamic battery behaviour. However, its accuracy in EV applications is impacted by time-varying parameters such as temperature, current, State of Charge (SoC), and State of Health (SoH). This study addresses the limitations of conventional RLS algorithms by proposing a systematic evaluation of seven Forgetting Factor Recursive Least Squares (FFRLS) methods under dynamic conditions. Two Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) assumptions-OCV as an estimated parameter and as a function of SoC-are considered. Using a second-order Thevenin model in MATLAB/Simulink and experimental data from a commercially available A123 battery, this work provides a comprehensive analysis of model accuracy and robustness. Key performance metrics, including mean absolute error (MAE), mean square error (MSE), root mean square error (RMSE), and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), validate the effectiveness of these methods. The findings offer valuable insights for improving dynamic parameter estimation in EV battery management.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of FFRLS Variants: Features and Advantages.

row.entry: Modifies the Nernst equation to include additional parameters for improved modelling.
Faster convergence and reduced identification error in online identification.
Utilizes a half-parabola curve relationship between voltage error and forgetting factor.
Adaptively adjusts the forgetting factor to minimize error and enhance stability.
Decouples the forgetting factors for each parameter to allow independent tracking.
Effectively handles distinct dynamics of parameters, improving estimation accuracy in dynamic environments.
Single-step correction improves parameter update speed by refining estimates after each iteration.
Achieves higher accuracy with minimal data requirements.
Multi-step correction applies several correction steps for each update cycle.
Further enhances convergence speed and stability, especially in highly dynamic systems.
Uses an adaptive window mode to determine the forgetting factor based on historical data differences.
Reduces the impact of abrupt changes or disturbances, improving stability and estimation accuracy.
Park’s VFFRLS introduces variability to the forgetting factor using recent data window theory.
Improves parameter estimation accuracy by dynamically adapting to system changes.
Alternative VFFRLS adapts the forgetting factor using error-weighted recursive techniques.
Balances sensitivity to sudden changes with robustness against noise.
Employs multiple forgetting factors optimized using Particle Swarm Optimization for each model parameter.
Improves state estimation by adapting to parameter-specific dynamics, suitable for scenarios with varied operating conditions.

head.ce:title: Improving dynamic parameter estimation in electric vehicle batteries: A comparative study of FFRLS methods with Open Circuit Voltage assumptions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate battery modelling is crucial for Battery Management Systems (BMS) to ensure precise state estimation and dynamic characteristic prediction, optimizing power and energy management in Electric Vehicles (EVs). Among various models, the Electric Equivalent Circuit Model (EECM) is widely used due to its ability to represent dynamic battery behaviour. However, its accuracy in EV applications is impacted by time-varying parameters such as temperature, current, State of Charge (SoC), and State of Health (SoH). This study addresses the limitations of conventional RLS algorithms by proposing a systematic evaluation of seven Forgetting Factor Recursive Least Squares (FFRLS) methods under dynamic conditions. Two Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) assumptions-OCV as an estimated parameter and as a function of SoC-are considered. Using a second-order Thevenin model in MATLAB/Simulink and experimental data from a commercially available A123 battery, this work provides a comprehensive analysis of model accuracy and robustness. Key performance metrics, including mean absolute error (MAE), mean square error (MSE), root mean square error (RMSE), and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), validate the effectiveness of these methods. The findings offer valuable insights for improving dynamic parameter estimation in EV battery management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para:
The reliance on fossil fuels in the transportation industry has led to increasing concerns about atmospheric pollution and global climate shifts. Gasoline or diesel powered vehicles release pollutants such as CO, HC, and
NO
x
along with greenhouse gases like (
CO
2
) [1,2]. Additionally, internal combustion engines (ICEs) are affected by oil price fluctuations and contribute to the depletion of the world’s limited oil reserves. These pressing issues have driven the popularity of Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs), Plug-in HEVs (PHEVs), and Full Electric Vehicles (FEVs) as they offer cleaner and lower-emission alternatives [3,4]. Electric vehicles are significantly more efficient – up to four times more so – and can be powered by renewable energy sources available locally [5,6].

There are many kinds of alternative energy sources to power vehicles, including batteries, supercapacitors, hydraulic and pneumatic reservoirs, and flywheels [7]. Despite being less expensive than electrical components, mechanical components have a relatively lower energy storage capacity. Lithium-Ion Batteries (LIBs) are considered the leading energy source for automotive applications. This is due to their high volumetric energy and power density, long lifespan, quick charging ability, and minimal self-discharge rate [8,9]. A Battery Management System (BMS) is essential for managing the battery in order to maintain the cells within safe operating limits, and to optimize the performance, e.g. range and power delivery. Furthermore, estimating key battery states- such as State of Charge (SoC), State of Health (SoH), State of Power (SoP), and State of Energy (SoE) - is one of the most essential functions of the BMS [10–12].

Accurate prediction of SoC and SoH by the BMS is crucial for enhancing system performance, reliability, and safety, as well as extending the battery’s lifespan. Precise estimation of the battery’s state helps avoid unexpected device shutdowns and prevents overcharging or excessive discharging, both of which can cause permanent damage to the battery’s internal structure [13]. Since the battery’s states cannot be measured directly, some cutting-edge techniques have been developed to provide reliable estimates. Methods for estimating the battery’s SoC can be classified into four categories [14]:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Open-loop methods include Coulomb-Counting algorithms, which rely on accurate current measurements along with knowledge of the battery’s capacity and initial SoC, as well as Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) methods, which require extended relaxation periods for the battery to achieve thermodynamic stability.
Model-based estimation methods that rely on electrochemical models and Electric Equivalent Circuit Models (EECMs).

Adaptive model-based methods, which include Kalman Filters (KFs), Extended Kalman Filters (EKFs), Unscented Kalman Filters (UKFs), Recursive Least Squares (RLS) methods, and unscented particle filters [15–17].

Data-driven estimation methods, for example, fuzzy logic, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Neural Network (NN) method, which are highly susceptible to external disturbances and depend on the battery dataset and accurate data acquisition [18].

ce:section.ce:para: The adaptive model-based approaches are the most widely used methods to estimate the SoC of the battery due to the ease of implementation and high precision. Evidently, the accuracy of these techniques relies on the accuracy of the underlying battery model [18].

The diverse array of battery models utilized to simulate the electrochemical dynamics of LIBs can be systematically categorized into four primary classifications, namely: Empirical Model (EM), Electrochemical Model (ECM), Electric Equivalent Circuit Model (EECM), and Electrochemical Impedance Model (ECIM) [18]. Empirical data fitting is used with EM for SoC estimation, and it has a low accuracy when attempting to depict battery dynamics [18]. Significant computational resources are necessary to solve the partial differential equations for ECM and ECIM. To capture the highly dynamic behaviour of the LIBs, the EECM uses electrical components such as a voltage source, resistors, and capacitors. In contrast to other categories, the EECM can more successfully balance estimating accuracy and computing efficiency. The battery EECMs include the Rint model [19], the Thevenin model [18], the Partnership for a New Generation of Vehicles (PNGV) model [20], the N-order RC model, and the Randles model [21]. The second-order RC model is particularly effective in capturing the battery’s non-linear characteristics, offering high estimation accuracy, suitability for online applications, and manageable computational demands. Studies have shown that this model can reduce the SoC estimation error to 2.3%, whereas the first-order RC model results in a significantly higher error of 6.2% [21,22]. The conventional Recursive Least Square (RLS) method is widely used for determining battery model parameters because it is simple to implement on microchips, requires minimal computation, and can accurately identify model parameters by periodically correcting uncertain signals, where each new estimate combines the previous estimate and a correction term. However, the traditional RLS algorithm has some limitations, particularly its high sensitivity to older error samples. As the algorithm processes large amounts of data recursively, its accuracy tends to decline over time due to the increasing influence of these older error samples. To address this issue, the Forgetting Factor-based RLS (FFRLS) algorithm introduces a forgetting factor that applies exponentially decreasing weights to older error samples [13,23]. This adjustment reduces the impact of older errors relative to more recent ones, allowing the algorithm to focus on more relevant data and converge more rapidly to the true value. By incorporating this forgetting factor, FFRLS improves the algorithm’s ability to adapt quickly to changes in dynamic environments. Ultimately, while traditional RLS struggles with the influence of older data and large datasets, FFRLS overcomes these challenges by prioritizing recent information, leading to faster convergence and better performance in real-time applications. It is important to note that achieving optimal forgetting factors involves balancing stability with accuracy. Frequent and substantial fluctuations in charging and discharging currents are common in EV applications, and such variability can negatively affect the precision and effectiveness of dynamic circuit parameter identification using the FFRLS method. This occurs due to the fact that the forgetting factor remains constant [13]. Therefore, various modified RLS algorithms have been developed in the literature, such as Variable FFRLS (VFFRLS) [24–26], Adaptive FFRLS (AFFRLS) [13,27], Decoupled Multiple FFRLS (DMFFRLS) [28], Modified Adaptive FFRLS (MAFFRLS) [23], Improved FFRLS (IFFRLS) [29–31], Sliding Window Difference FFRLS (SDFFRLS) [32] and Multiple FFRLS (MFFRLS) [33]. Table 1 summarizes the key points of the different variants of RLS-based algorithms, while Table 2 shows the key features and the advantges of each method.

Based on the previous discussion and to the best knowledge of the authors, most of the aforementioned RLS-based algorithms are benchmarked against the conventional RLS or FFRLS methods [23,25,27–29]. Furthermore, some studies are only presented on first-order RC EECM and the methods have not been compared against each other. Therefore, this paper aims to address this gap in the literature by presenting a critical comparison among seven approaches, namely: FFRLS [33], VFFRLS [24–26], AFFRLS [13], MAFFRLS [23] and DMFFRLS [28], and the performance criteria are the Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Square Error (MSE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of the terminal voltage error. The comparison is performed on a second-order RC EECM with two different assumptions of OCV implementation. The first scenario assumes the OCV is parameter to be estimated as the sampling time is very small, while the second considers the OCV as a function in the battery SoC.
The main contribution of this paper can be summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: A review of the previous variant RLS-based algorithms used for battery 1RC and 2RC EECM parameters estimation.
A critical comparison between different RLS-based algorithms for 2RC EECM under constant OCV and OCV as a function in SoC assumptions.
Optimal tuning for each method parameter using particle swarm optimization to make the comparison fair and objective.

ce:section.ce:para: The structure of the paper is organized as follows: Section 2 details the second-order RC electric equivalent circuit model for batteries and the necessary discretization for its implementation in a BMS. Section 3 outlines the analytical method for estimating the parameters of the second-order RC model, while Section 4 discusses the fundamentals of the RLS method. In Section 5, the algorithms VFFRLS, AFFRLS, MAFFRLS, DMFFRLS, and IFFRLS are introduced. Section 6 provides criteria for comparing the performance of various RLS-based approaches. The simulation results and comparisons are presented in Section 7, followed by concluding remarks in Section 8.

Two assumptions can be drawn from (4) as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: OCV as a Function of SoC: Here, the OCV is expressed as a predefined function of the SoC, typically obtained from experimental data and represented as an OCV-SoC curve. This approach is ideal for scenarios where the OCV-SoC relationship is stable and accurately mapped, reducing the computational load by avoiding real-time OCV estimation.

OCV as an Estimated Parameter: In this approach, OCV is treated as a parameter to be estimated in real time. This is based on the assumption that the sampling time is very small, making it feasible to continuously update OCV as part of the recursive estimation process. This method is particularly suitable for scenarios where real-time adaptability is required, such as dynamic drive cycles with rapidly changing conditions. Therefore,
U
o c
is taken as a parameter to be identified and (4) can be rearranged as follows:

The key difference between these methods lies in their trade-off between real-time adaptability and computational efficiency. The first approach is computationally less intensive and relies on pre-characterized data, while the second provides more dynamic responsivenes. Both methods have been validated under different driving profiles, such as DST and US06, to highlight their applicability and performance in various operating conditions.

It is possible to obtain the EECM parameters numerically. In this study, a commercially available A123 battery is tested and Table 3 lists its specifications [4]. The setup described in Fig. 2. The battery was subjected to a pulse discharge test at room temperature (hybrid pulse power characterization test) to get the EECM parameters. The battery was fully charged using Constant Current Constant Voltage (CCCV) protocol from a lower cut-off of 2.7 V up to the cut-off voltage of 4.2 V under the ambient temperature of
20 °

When the battery is entirely discharged, or when its voltage falls below the discharge cut-off voltage (2.7 V as shown in Table 3), the discharge operation comes to an end. Throughout the whole test, sensor signals such as current and voltage, were recorded at one second sampling intervals. The corresponding terminal voltage curve is shown in Fig. 3. Where

is the actual observation value of the system. The weight of new and old sample errors in RLS method are modified by a forgetting factor λ (where 0
< λ <
1) that usually varies from 0.95 to 0.99 [30]. When λ is set to a small value, the weight assigned to new error samples increases, enhancing the system’s tracking performance. However, this also results in greater fluctuations in the parameter identification outcomes. Conversely, a larger λ increases the weight of older error samples, which reduces the system’s tracking effectiveness but results in less fluctuation in the parameter identification outcomes. The forgetting factor recursive least squares algorithm requires enhancement, as it can be susceptible to unexpected oscillations and even divergence. Fig. 5 depicts the variation of estimated voltage error with λ ranging from 0.95 to 1. It can be observed that for all values of the forgetting factor λ , the error tends to be high at the beginning and end of the sample set due to improper initial value settings. However, in between these periods, the error is relatively small. As λ increases from 0.96 to 1, the algorithm gives less weight to new data, resulting in decreased system tracking performance and a decrease in voltage error.
In this section, each different RLS- based algorithms i.e. VVFFRLS, AFFRLS, MAFFRLS, DMFFRLS, and IFFRLS to improve the performance of FFRLS algorithms is briefly outlined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sliding window difference forgetting factor RLS
ce:section.ce:para:
The approach described in [26] has two disadvantages. Firstly, the forgetting factor is determined by individual data errors, which can result in abrupt changes when inaccurate data or disturbances occur, leading to increased subsequent error estimation and instability in the results. Additionally, when the terminal voltage error is relatively small during the parameter identification process, the value of λ remains at 1, and
L
( k )
consistently equals 0, resulting in an inability to accurately identify the parameters. Typically, there are two strategies to resolve the aforementioned issue: either by adjusting the sensitivity factor ρ or by eliminating the rounding function
r o u n d
( x )
. To address the issues of VFFRLS algorithms in [25,26], a data analysis approach is proposed in [32]. The mean square value of previous
( k − d + 1 )

To estimate the parameter of the first-order RC electric equivalent circuit model of a LIB, an adaptive forgetting factor recursive least square method is provided. To achieve faster convergence and reduce identification error in online identification, the forgetting factor should adaptively adjust based on the identification parameter error, particularly when the parameter error is significantly large during the online identification process [27]. The following modified equation is suggested to compute the adaptive forgetting factor to make the forgetting factor change adaptively:

Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is employed to find the optimal initial parameter value and forgetting factor of the FFRLS algorithm that is screened in real time. The absolute value of the terminal voltage error is chosen to be the objective function [31]. The detailed process of optimizing the FFRLS algorithm using PSO is outlined in Table 4.

In [29] an enhanced RLS algorithm is proposed that incorporates multiple corrections of the estimated parameter vector
θ
ˆ
( k )
to address the shortcomings of the conventional RLS algorithm, all without requiring additional data. The concept behind this algorithm involves incorporating an inner loop into the conventional RLS algorithm. This inner loop conducts iterative calculations of the estimated parameter vector for M iterations, enabling the battery parameters to be updated rapidly and in real-time. During the M iterations within the inner loop, the gain vector
K
( k )
, the information vector
ϕ
( k )
and the actual observation value
y
( k )
remain constant, with only the estimated parameter vector
θ
ˆ
( k )
being updated across the M iterations. Within the inner loop, as the iterative process progresses, the estimated parameter vector
θ
ˆ
i n
( m − 1 )
approaches the true value. Consequently, the difference between the predicted data
ϕ
T
( k )
θ
ˆ
i n
( m − 1 )
and the actual data
y
( k )
decreases. This leads to a more effective correction and enhances the accuracy of the parameter identification results. Derived from the previously discussed concept, the detailed steps for the algorithm used to identify the battery parameters are presented in Algorithm 1.

The standard forgetting factor RLS is susceptible to irregular fluctuations and can even diverge, highlighting the need for enhancements to this algorithm. Consequently, an improved version of the FFRLS is proposed in [30]. The mean μ and standard deviation σ of the identification results from the initial 100 iterations are calculated. Based on these values, the upper boundary of the identification result is dynamically established as
μ + 3 σ
, while the lower boundary is determined as the greater of 0 or
μ − 3 σ
. If the identification result at discrete time k falls outside the established boundary values, it will be adjusted to the nearest boundary value. Subsequently, the terminal voltage error is calculated using the battery model parameters subject to dynamic constraints. If the terminal voltage error is less than 0.05 V, the identification result will be outputted. If it exceeds this threshold, a 40-step parameter backtracking will be executed, and the identification result that minimizes the terminal voltage error during this backtracking will be provided. The flow chart of improved forgetting factor recursive least squares is shown in Fig. 9.

The parameters of the EECM for the battery fluctuate due to several factors, including operating temperature, SoC, and SoH. Additionally, these parameters typically change at different rates. For instance, the internal resistance
R
0
of the battery is affected by temperature, SoC, and SoH, whereas the polarization resistance
R
p
is significantly influenced by the rapidly changing load current. Consequently, using fixed values for the forgetting factor leads to unreliable state estimations, particularly in situations where the battery’s surrounding environment is subject to change. The RLS algorithm incorporating multiple forgetting factors has been suggested to address this requirement. In [33], individual forgetting factors were assigned to each parameter, enabling the algorithm to effectively capture the distinct dynamics associated with those parameters. Particle Swarm Optimisation was employed to identify the optimal forgetting factors. The proposed method can be described by the following equations:

A method known as Decoupling Multiple Forgetting Factors Recursive Least Squares (DMFFRLS) has been proposed for the identification of EV battery parameters, addressing the challenges associated with the FFRLS approach [28]. The errors associated with various parameters are differentiated, allowing for independent tracking of each parameter while considering the distinct physical characteristics of the battery parameters [28]. Moreover, decoupling multiple forgetting factors is employed to update the covariance matrix, reflecting the varying degrees of error associated with each parameter during the identification process. ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation and comparative analysis of the performance in identifying model parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
The parameters’ actual values are unknown. As a result, the estimated value generated from the identification parameters is compared to the observed terminal voltage value, and the terminal voltage error is used as a criterion to assess the accuracy of parameter identification. The proposed algorithms are given from four perspectives to properly assess their performance: mean absolute error (MAE), mean square error (MSE), root mean square error (RMSE), and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) [37,38].

, and

denote the estimated and measured values terminal voltage or SoC values, while N represents the total number of samples.

To assess the various RLS-based identification algorithms under both OCV assumptions, experimental data from HPPC and OCV tests conducted on a 20Ah commercially available LFP pouch cell provided by A123 at 100% SoH is utilized to model the 2RC EECM, with its specifications detailed in Table 3. In order to accurately represent the relationship between the battery’s OCV and SoC at various temperatures, this study employs an 8th-order polynomial for analysing the battery OCV, as illustrated in Fig. 10. It should be noted that, the analysis in this study focuses on the 80% to 20% SoC range. Extrapolated OCV values outside this range, particularly negative values, are the results of the curve fitting process due to the lack of experimental data below 5% SoC. The 2RC EECM parameters and SoC estimation algorithms are validated online using MATLAB Simulink, the general structure of the joint estimation is illustrated in Fig. 11. Here the different methods are tested at 25
C
o
under DST and US06 driving profiles. The PSO algorithm is used to optimize the initial covariance matrix and parameters of the FFRLS-based algorithms. Specifically, PSO helps determine the optimal starting values for the covariance and parameters across different drive cycles, such as DST and US06. The optimization process focuses on minimizing the MSE between the estimated and measured terminal voltages, which are obtained from the 2RC EECM of the battery. This is done while simulating a real-time scenario where the battery is discharged from 80% to 20% of its full capacity so that the comparison can be fair [39]. The corresponding optimal values assuming OCV as a function of SoC and OCV as a parameter are given in Tables 5 and 6.

The battery is discharged from 80% to 20% to mimic a real driving situation. Figs. 12 and 13 show the simulation results of the estimated EECM parameters with assuming OCV as a function of SoC. The DST and US06 current profiles are applied to discharge the battery from 80% to 20%. It should be mentioned that the EKF is used to estimate the SoC and compared against the Coulomb counting law as shown in Fig. 12(g) and (h). It can be seen that, the DMVFFRLS and VFFRLS [24] provide higher variation in the polarized elements i.e
R
1
,
R
2
,
C
1
and
C
2
(less stability) in the estimated parameters in both current profiles compared to other methods. From Figs. 12(h) and 13(h), the MAFFRLS provides less SoC error, while VFFRLS [24] gives a higher one. Table 7 presents metrics results for various estimation methods applied to two different profiles: DST and US06. Among the methods, AFFRLS demonstrates relatively high error rates for both profiles, with an MAE of 1.5274 and MSE of 3.2028e−7 for the DST profile, and an MAE of 1.417 and MSE of 1.8063e−7 for the US06 profile. FFRLS generally outperforms AFFRLS and achieves notably lower MAE and MSE values, particularly evident in the US06 profile where FFRLS achieves a remarkably low MAE of 0.0126 and MSE of 1.1710e−8. MAFFRLS and MVFFRLS show competitive performance with relatively low MAE and MSE values across both profiles. VFFRLS [25] and VFFRLS [26] consistently exhibit lower MAE and MSE values compared to AFFRLS and FFRLS, indicating superior performance. However, VFFRLS presents varying performance with substantially higher error rates for the DST profile and relatively lower error rates for the US06 profile. Overall, the results highlight the strengths of FFRLS and the referenced methods in achieving lower error rates, particularly for the US06 profile, emphasizing the importance of method selection based on specific profile characteristics and performance metrics.

Figs. 14 and 15 demonstrate the results with OCV as a parameter assumption. Similar to OCV function in SoC case, the DMFFRLS provides higher variation and less stability and VFFRLS [24] is unstable with DST and US06 current profiles at lower SoC values. This because the parameter R has a constant value during the discharge profile and at lower SoC values, the terminal voltage tends to drop rapidly due to the battery’s discharge characteristics. Therefore, the model cannot accurately capture these dynamics and this makes the error
e
k
large. Table 8 displays metrics results for various estimation methods, encompassing AFFRLS, FFRLS, MAFFRLS, DMFFRLS, VFFRLS [25], VFFRLS [26], and VFFRLS [24], evaluated on both DST and US06 profiles under OCV as a parameter assumption. For AFFRLS, while it achieves relatively low MAE values for the DST profile (0.0998), the results are less favourable for the US06 profile (1.3673), indicating a significant discrepancy in performance between the two profiles. Conversely, FFRLS demonstrates more consistent performance across profiles, with slightly higher MAE values for DST (0.1200) and lower values for US06 (1.0538). MAFFRLS also exhibits relatively consistent performance, though with higher MAE values for US06 (0.4847) compared to DST (0.1147). DMFFRLS, however, shows considerable disparity in performance between the two profiles with significantly higher MAE values for US06 (82.3591) compared to DST (0.2067). Notably, VFFRLS demonstrates varying degrees of performance, with considerably higher MAE values for the DST profile across different references and scenarios compared to US06. Specifically, VFFRLS [24] exhibits exceptionally high MAE values for the DST profile (1.8936e3), indicating potential challenges in handling certain profiles or scenarios. Overall, the table underscores the importance of method selection based on profile characteristics and the necessity of robustness assessment across different scenarios for accurate estimation.

This study comprehensively evaluated several advanced RLS-based algorithms, including FFRLS, VFFRLS, MAFFRLS, AFFRLS, and DMFFRLS, for second-order RC electric equivalent circuit model LIB parameter estimation. The analysis covered model discretization and parameter identification, providing a clear methodology for the proposed approaches. Under two distinct scenarios – OCV as an estimated parameter and OCV as a function of SoC – the seven algorithms were assessed using MAE, MSE, RMSE, and MAPE metrics. For the DST profile with OCV as a function of SoC, VFFRLS developed by Q.Song et al. (2019), achieved the highest accuracy (RMSE: 26.123 μ V), while MAFFRLS outperformed other methods for the US06 profile with an RMSE of 0.84794 mV. Conversely, for the scenario with OCV as a parameter to be estimated, the VFFRLS method provided the best results for the DST profile (RMSE: 63.97 μ V), and MAFFRLS again excelled for the US06 profile. These results highlight significant variations in algorithm performance across different conditions and current profiles. The investigation reveals that the IFFRLS algorithm offers a balanced trade-off between identification accuracy, robustness, and convergence for both OCV assumptions. Moreover, the study recommends using the OCV as a function of SoC assumption when OCV-SoC curves are available, as it consistently delivers higher accuracy across all tested methods. This critical comparison emphasizes the importance of selecting estimation methods based on the availability of battery-specific data, parameter tuning complexity, and operational profile characteristics. In summary, this research provides a robust framework for LIB parameter identification, demonstrating that adaptive algorithms can significantly enhance the accuracy and reliability of EECM in dynamic EV environments. Future work will focus on implementing IFFRLS for online parameter identification combined with Unscented Kalman Filters to achieve more precise SoC and SoH estimations, further advancing BMS performance.
